coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detection of histamine based on specific binding bioreceptors and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Rongjia
Feng, Shuaihui
Park, Chan Yeong
Park, Kyung Yeol
Song, Jeonghun
Park, Jong Pil
Chun, Hyang Sook
Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: Histamine is primarily found in spoiled food and often used as an indicator of food safety. Compared to various existing methods for analyzing histamine, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) which is accurate but time-consuming, and immunochemical methods that are difficult to produce high specificity and affinity antibodies towards small molecules have been used. In this study, we developed a newly designed, sensitive, and selective fluorescence detection platform for histamine sensing, utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and synthetic peptides. Specifically, through biopanning approaches, a series of peptides having a high affinity towards immobilized histamine hapten were selected from phage-displayed libraries. Then, CQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment enabling their fluorescence to be effectively quenched by peptides via the electron transfer interactions. While, in the presence of histamine, fluorescence will be recovered because of the stronger interaction between peptide and target. In this study, from the selectivity tests towards histamine and in contrast to structurally similar compounds, peptide Hisp3 (DIDRAGKASHWP) along with its dipeptide repeat derivative (Hisp3-2-C) were chemically synthesized to be used as promising histamine receptors. Furthermore, the application of peptide along with gold-coated magnetic nanoparticles (MNP@Au NPs) was designed for purification and analysis of fish samples. These results indicate that the CQDs and peptide sensor system could detect histamine at lower concentrations with high sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Histamine
Carbon quantum dots
Phage display
Specific peptide bioreceptor
Fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of histamine hapten for phage display
Synthesis of His-SA as a histamine hapten
Selection of the best peptide by fluorescence detection platform
Magnetic separation and fluorescence detection in real sample
Interaction between NAC-CQDs and histamine binding peptide candidates
Selectivity test to evaluate candidate peptides
Sensitivity test to evaluate candidate peptides

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of the obtained CDs. (b) 2D topographical contour map of NAC-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of NAC-CQDs in the presence of (a) different concentrations of histamine; in the presence of different peptides (b) Hisp1, (b) Hisp2, (c) Hisp3, and (d) Hisp4 adding 100 ppm histamine.

Selectivity analysis, fluorescence intensity recovery (F–F0) of NAC-CQDs after adding (a) Hisp1, (b) Hisp2, (c) Hisp3, (d) Hisp4 (F represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of histamine, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence of histamine).

Sensitivity tests. The linear response of the fluorescence recovery of the NAC-CQDs under different concentrations of histamine in the presence of peptides, (a) Hisp1, (b) Hisp2, (c) Hisp3, and (d) Hisp4.
(a) Schematic diagram of CQDs-based validation test of the candidate peptide. (b) Schematic illustration of the proposed assay for histamine detection in real sample.
Comparison of analytical performance with other previously reported methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Abbreviations: MS, Mass spectrometry; GC, Gas chromatography; LC, Liquid chromatography; TANDEM MS, Tandem mass spectrometry; TLC, Thin layer chromatography; FIA, Flow injection analysis; IEC, Ion exchange chromatography; FRET, Fluorescence resonance energy transfer; HB, Histamine blue; FC, Fluorescence sensor; DAO, Diamine oxidase; HRP, Horseradish peroxidase; MNP, Magnetic nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detection of histamine based on specific binding bioreceptors and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology, School of Food Science and Technology, Chung-Ang University, 4726 Seodong-daero, Anseong 17546, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology, School of Food Science and Technology, Chung-Ang University, 4726 Seodong-daero, Anseong 17546, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Histamine is primarily found in spoiled food and often used as an indicator of food safety. Compared to various existing methods for analyzing histamine, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) which is accurate but time-consuming, and immunochemical methods that are difficult to produce high specificity and affinity antibodies towards small molecules have been used. In this study, we developed a newly designed, sensitive, and selective fluorescence detection platform for histamine sensing, utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and synthetic peptides. Specifically, through biopanning approaches, a series of peptides having a high affinity towards immobilized histamine hapten were selected from phage-displayed libraries. Then, CQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment enabling their fluorescence to be effectively quenched by peptides via the electron transfer interactions. While, in the presence of histamine, fluorescence will be recovered because of the stronger interaction between peptide and target. In this study, from the selectivity tests towards histamine and in contrast to structurally similar compounds, peptide Hisp3 (DIDRAGKASHWP) along with its dipeptide repeat derivative (Hisp3-2-C) were chemically synthesized to be used as promising histamine receptors. Furthermore, the application of peptide along with gold-coated magnetic nanoparticles (MNP@Au NPs) was designed for purification and analysis of fish samples. These results indicate that the CQDs and peptide sensor system could detect histamine at lower concentrations with high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine (β-imidazolylethylamine) being biogenic in nature is a key mediator of various physiological and pathological processes in the human body. High levels of histamine in the human body can bind to G protein-coupled histamine receptors, thereby initiating several allergic-related inflammatory diseases (Huang and Thurmond, 2008). It is a highly conserved autacoid throughout most vertebrate tissues (Panula et al., 2015). In addition, it naturally occurs at higher levels in certain species of fish, however, in some fermented food, it can be produced by the enzymatic decarboxylation of histidine through bacterial action (Doeun et al., 2017). Generally, it is hard to recognize food spoilage initially as appearance looks normal, hence, histamine level can act as one of the significant indicators of food quality. Thus, developing a rapid and sensitive method for histamine detection is essential for maintaining food safety.

Various methods including thin-layer chromatography (TLC), gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical sensing, capillary zone electrophoresis, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) have been constantly employed for the detection of histamine (Hwang et al., 2003; Lieber and Taylor, 1978; Luo et al., 2014). However, these conventional technologies have several inherent drawbacks such as high cost, difficulty in transportation due to a huge size, qualified analysts required to handle, and laborious (Gagic et al., 2019). Recently, with the advent of modern technologies, analytical devices such as chemical and biological sensors have changed the scenario of identification of analyte. These analytical devices, mostly comprised of a physical transducer coupled to a specific bio-probe (e.g., antibodies, receptors, peptides, etc.) have made the rapid and specific detection of the targets possible, and they have been widely used in clinical diagnostics, several foods, and environmental applications. During the past decades, antibodies have been usually chosen to detect various chemical entities due to their sensitivity and selectivity. For instance, aptamer-based photoluminescence immunosensors showed the highest sensitivity towards Ochratoxin A (Myndrul et al., 2017). However, the production of antibodies with high specificity and affinity towards small molecules is difficult; for example, haptens are challenging to synthesis owing to their low molecular weight and simple structure. Recent studies indicate the successful use of peptides due to their smaller size and better flexibility to capture small molecules (Hwang et al., 2015). Phage display is one of the powerful molecular display techniques useful for high throughput screening of protein interactions. It is primarily used to select high-affinity target-binding peptides from a complex mixture pool of displayed peptides, which could be further enriched through the biopanning process (Sarikaya et al., 2003). Therefore, using phage display technology, screening, and selection of peptides and selectively binding against histamine with high affinity and specificity is achievable.

Nowadays, optical biosensor platforms have attracted much attention. There are many kinds of optical biosensors based on absorbance, photoluminescence, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) as well as a fluorescence for research (Myndrul et al., 2018; Oh and Altug, 2018). As one of the most prevailing strategies, several fluorescence-based histamine detection methods had been reported (Feng et al., 2018). To enhance the selectivity and efficiency of the histamine detection biosensor, here we established a new platform based on peptide and carbon quantum dots to select the peptide which has the best affinity to histamine. Firstly, peptides were thoroughly screened through phage display technology for enhancing specificity. Furthermore, the carbon quantum dots were prepared, capped with N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) with high stability and photoluminescence. As shown in Scheme 1 a, the fluorescence intensity of NAC-CQDs was quenched by peptides firstly but recovered with the histamine added. Furthermore, as the complexity of the matrix sample, separation histamine from food samples shows a significant challenge. In this study, after getting the most promising affinity peptide, a cysteine-incorporated peptide was synthesized and combined with a magnetic nanoplatform to detect the target in a real sample (Scheme 1b).

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC), gold (III) chloride hydrate, N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), tryptamine, spermidine, agmatine, tyramine, 2-phenethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine, and spermine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Histamine (His), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ammonium hydroxide, and succinic anhydride (SA) were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). SMART™ BCA protein assay kit was purchased from iNtRON Biotechnology (Seongnam, Korea). Agarose was purchased from Takara Bio (Shiga, Japan). Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals were of reagent grade and prepared using deionized (DI) water from Direct-Q® Water Purification System (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).

Escherichia coli strain ER2738 was used in this study for amplification, titration, and DNA purification. Unless otherwise stated, it was cultured on Luria-Bertani (LB) agar medium containing tetracycline (100 μg/mL) at 37 °C for overnight. For amplification or titration, single colonies were inoculated into LB medium [peptone (1%); yeast extract (0.5%); NaCl (0.5%) (w/v) in DI water] at 37 °C with shaking until log phase of growth was reached. Ph.D. 7™ and Ph.D. 12™ phage-display peptide library kits were obtained from New England BioLabs (Ipswich, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of histamine hapten for phage display
Synthesis of His-SA as a histamine hapten
ce:section.ce:para: SA (0.59 g, 5.94 mmol) was dissolved into 10 mL DMF under N2 atmosphere at room temperature. Subsequently, histamine (0.60 g, 5.40 mmol) in 20 mL DMF was injected dropwise into the above solution, followed by stirring for 6 h. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation, and 50 mL of ethanol (EtOH) was poured over it to crystallize the product. It was first stored at 4 °C for overnight, then filtered, followed by washing with cold EtOH. The filtrate was dried under room temperature, and His-SA (hapten) was obtained as a white solid with a yield of 82.1%.

The hapten (20.0 mg, 0.095 mmol) was dissolved in 1 mL DMF, followed by addition of NHS (12.0 mg, 0.10 mmol) at 4 °C. After stirring for 10 min, DCC (21.0 mg, 0.125 mmol) was added. The blended solution was refrigerated at 4 °C for 15 h. After activating the hapten with DCC and NHS, it was filtered to remove dicyclohexylurea. The filtrate was gently added to a solution of BSA (28 mg) in DI water (2 mL) with stirring at 4 °C. After 15 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 15,814×g for 10 min, followed by dialysis of the supernatant for three days. The obtained BSA-haptens was then verified by MALDI-TOF analysis.

The schematic representation of the biopanning strategy phage display peptide libraries is described in Fig. S1. 96-well immunoplates (SPL Life Sciences, Pocheon, Korea) were coated with BSA and BSA-hapten at 100 μg/mL in 0.1 M NaHCO3 (pH 8.6) and incubated it overnight at 4 °C. These plates were then washed three times with TBST (50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5) + 150 mM NaCl + 0.1% Tween 20), followed by blocking with prepared blocking buffer (0.1 M NaHCO3, 5 mg/mL BSA, pH 8.6) for 2 h at 4 °C. The plates were further washed using TBST for six times. The respective peptide library (1 × 1011 pfu/mL) was added to the pre-immobilized wells and incubated them for 40 min at 25 °C. To remove unbound phages, the plates were washed 10 times with TBST solution. Three rounds of biopanning were performed against BSA and BSA-hapten. After washing, the bound phages were eluted by incubating it with 100 μL of 0.2 M glycine- HCl (pH 2.2) for 20 min at 25 °C. The eluent was then immediately neutralized with 15 μL of 1.0 M Tris-HCl (pH 9.1) solution to prevent degradation of the phages.

The eluted phages were amplified for further use by inoculating 1.0 μL of phage suspension to 20 mL LB medium containing 0.1 mg/mL tetracycline and 200 μL of overnight E. coli strain ER2738 culture, followed by incubation for no longer than 5 h at 37 °C. The culture was then centrifuged at 18,341 ×g for 30 s at 4 °C. 80% of supernatant was transferred to a fresh tube with 200 μL of PEG 8000/2.5 M NaCl and incubated for 15–60 min on ice. After 20 min, it was centrifuged at 18,341 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was discarded, and the pellet was resuspended in 100 μL iodide buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0, 1 mM EDTA, 4.0 M sodium iodide and absolute ethyl alcohol), followed by incubation for 20 min at room temperature. The solution was then centrifuged at 14,000 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was discarded. The pellet was further washed with 70% EtOH and centrifuged again. The pellet was resuspended in TE buffer (10 nM Tris pH 8.0, 1.0 mM EDTA) at −20 °C. DNA sequencing was performed by Cosmo Genetech (Seoul, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the best peptide by fluorescence detection platform

ce:section.ce:para: NAC (0.08 g, 0.5 mmol) and citric acid (0.13 g, 0.68 mmol) was dissolved in 8 mL DI water and sonicated for 15 min, followed by addition of ammonium hydroxide solution dropwise to adjust the pH value to 7.0. It was later transferred to 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed at 200 °C in a muffle furnace for 3 h. Following cool-down to room temperature, the samples were put the obtained black paste dissolved in 15 mL DI water and centrifuged at 11,323 ×g for 15 min to remove the insoluble matter. Finally, it was filtered using 0.25 μM syringe filters to remove the large dots, and then dialyzed against DI water for 24 h. It was stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

For the fluorescence detection of histamine, 50 μL of different candidate peptides (0.1 mg/mL) was added into 100 μL of NAC-CQDs, followed by the addition of 50 μL of different concentrations of histamine ranging from 0.1 to 100 ppm. After an incubation period for 5 min, the fluorescence recovery at 450 nm was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Selectivity tests were also performed by similar experiments by replacing histamine with different biogenic amines like tryptamine, spermidine, agmatine, tyramine, 2-phenethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine, and spermine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic separation and fluorescence detection in real sample

ce:section.ce:para: A fish matrix is very complex and has a huge auto-fluorescence background. Hence, separating histamine from the unknown fish sample is essential. Real sample detection is illustrated in Scheme 1b. Gold-coated magnet nanocomposites (MNP@Au) were functionalized by cysteine-incorporated peptide (MNP@Au@peptide), which can selectively capture histamine from the sample solution. Then, MNP@Au@peptide with histamine were separated from the samples by a magnetic field, and after washing with DI water for three times. Then introduced them to the previous fluorescence platform. After separation from the mixed solution by a magnetic field, get the emission fluorescence recovery of NAC-CQDs.

MNP@Au NPs were synthesized by a previous method (Calatayud et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015). The thiol-modified dual-repeated peptide, Hisp3-2-C, was conjugated to MNP@Au via Au–S covalent bond between Au surface and peptide. Briefly, MNP@Au (0.1 mg/mL) were incubated with Hisp3-2-C (0.5 mg/mL) for 2 h in PBS (pH 7.4) at room temperature. The resulting conjugates MNP@Au@Hisp3-2-C were washed with DI water more than 5 times under a high magnetic field, and the final concentration of the conjugates is 0.5 mg/mL.

Histamine extraction from mackerel fish was followed by a previous method (Mao et al., 2019). For quantitative histamine analysis, MNP@Au@Hisp3-2-C were used, and calibration was achieved by external standardization. Firstly, histamine was diluted in real black samples, which exclude histamine to various concentrations from 1 to 640 ppm. Then, added 1.0 mL MNP@Au@Hisp3-2-C to 1.0 mL standard solution and incubation 20 min to ensure the conjugation, after which use a magnetic field to separate and dispersed them into 1.0 mL DI water. Then, 50 μL of Hisp3 (0.1 mg/mL) was added into 100 μL of NAC-CQDs, followed by the addition of 50 μL of the purified real sample, incubation for 20 min, using a magnetic field to separate and get a linear curve between fluorescence and histamine concentrations. A similar procedure was used for a quantitative unknown real sample. Lastly, their concentrations were calculated from the standard curve. The results of the HPLC analysis were compared to demonstrate the feasibility of the method.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Varian Unity VNS 600 NMR spectrometer (1H and 13C at 600 MHz, Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA). Gas chromatography/high-resolution mass spectrometry (GC/HRMS) was carried out using a MS-700 (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) mounted with a gas chromatograph (6890N, Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The MALDI-TOF mass spectra were recorded on a MALDI-TOF voyager DE-STR (Applied Biosystems). The zeta potential and DLS measurements were completed on a particle size analyzer ELSZ-1000 system (Photal OTSUKA Electronics, Tokyo, Japan). FT-IR spectrum was measured by using FT-IR spectrometer (Jasco 6600FV, Jasco, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence intensity was recorded on a multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek Synergy H1, Winooski, VT, USA). HR-TEM image was obtained by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, 300 kV, JEM-3010, Jeol). Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was used. The quantum yield of NAC-CQDs was calculated by using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) (Zhu et al., 2013).

The composition of the His-SA (hapten 1) is described in Fig. S2a. (see Supporting Information) Based on the structural characterization data obtained from 1H NMR (Fig. S2b), 13C NMR (Fig. S2c), and GC/HRMS. It was confirmed that the hapten 1 was successfully synthesized. HRMS (EI) m/z was calculated for C9H14N3O3 [M+H] 212.1039, found 212.1035.1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 600 MHz) δ: 2.30 (t, J = 7.02 Hz, 2H), 2.42 (t, J = 6.98 Hz, 2H), 2.61 (t, J = 7.49 Hz, 2H), 3.21–3.27 (m, 2H), 6.78 (s, 1H), 7.53 (d, J = 1.12, 1H), 7.91 (t, J = 5.7 Hz, 1H). 13C NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 600 MHz) δ: 27.36; 29.70 (2C); 30.54; 40.25 (2C); 117.33; 135.00; 171.26; 174.32.

The association of BSA with hapten 1 (BSA-hapten) was further verified by MALDI-TOF, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The molecular weight (MW) of BSA is 66.2 kDa, and it was similarly represented in the MALDI-TOF spectrum. The BSA-hapten peak was observed at 67.8 kDa, which suggested the successful binding of haptens onto BSA. As the MW of one hapten is 211.22 Da, the number of haptens conjugated to BSA was predicted to be approximately 7.6.

Three rounds of biopanning were performed by using BSA-hapten and Ph.D. 7™ and Ph.D. 12™ phage-display peptide library. The overall phage recovery ratio was increased as it progressed (Table S1). After each round of biopanning, phages were eluted, DNA extracted, followed by sequencing. Each phage retains its own unique DNA sequence, which can be yielded to the candidate peptide information. Table S2 shows the sequence information of the eluted phages. Among them, ten clones of the peptide sequence ‘QVPESRH’ were obtained during three rounds of biopanning. Similarly, two clones of the peptide sequence ‘SAHPSRVNVHSL’ were reported during biopanning. Besides, similar sequences, ‘DIDRAGKASHWP’ and ‘DIARTAKPSHWP’, were screened. Therefore, four candidates, which were repeatedly from different rounds of biopanning, were selected (their names and characterization are summarized in Table S3) and synthesized for further verification of their affinity towards histamine.

Hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and NAC obtained a brown dispersion of NAC-CQDs that showed good water solubility and no precipitation or agglomeration at room temperature within two months duration. The quantum yield of NAC-CQDs was calculated to be 19.4% by using quinine sulfate as a reference standard.

The optical properties of NAC-CQDs were investigated through fluorescence spectra. Fig. 1 a shows its excitation and emission spectra of NAC-CQDs in water. The excitation independent photoluminescence property of NAC-CQDs is represented by 2D topographical contour map in Fig. 1b. The maximum excitation wavelength is observed to be 360 nm when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 400 nm, whereas, the maximum emission wavelength of the NAC-CQDs aqueous solution is found to be 450 nm. The strong photoluminescence emission may be attributed to the S atoms and N atoms co-doped on the surface of NAC-CQDs.

In order to investigate the phase structure and crystallinity of the synthesized NAC-CQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted. Fig. S4a shows the XRD pattern of NAC-CQDs, which confirms the presence of broad peaks at 2θ = 25°, and it is attributed to the amorphous nature of NAC-CQDs. In comparison, the XRD pattern of prepared NAC-CQDs has a broader peak than graphite and graphene that attributed to the smaller crystalline size of NAC-CQDs (Li et al., 2019). Also, FT-IR spectra of prepared NAC-CQDs are shown in Fig. S4b. The peak at 1068 cm−1 was assigned to the C–O bonds (Lieber and Taylor, 1978). The peaks at 1714.55 cm−1 and 1088.12 cm−1 are attributed to COO− groups (Liu et al., 2012). C–O–C stretching vibrations show at 1350.08 cm−1. In addition, the broad band at 3433.42 cm−1 appeared due to the presence of OH and NH2 groups. It can be observed that CDs exhibited bands at 2930 cm−1 due to C–H stretch vibrations (Luo et al., 2014). The typical TEM micrograph of the NAC-CQDs is shown in Fig. S5a. And, no aggregation was observed, indicating the good dispersibility of NAC-CQDs in water. The HRTEM (right) image of the NAC-CQDs reveals that they are amorphous in nature, as there is no well-resolved lattice plane (Fig. S5b). The DLS data showed that the particle sizes of NAC-CQDs are above 3.3 nm (Fig. S5c). Furthermore, the zeta potential of CDs is −37.75 mV as shown in Fig. S6. As the pKa value of the stabilizer NAC is 3.24, NAC-CQDs possess strong negative surface charges at neutral pH, which indicates from carboxyl deprotonation. Cysteamine (CA) and L-cys (L-cys), used as stabilizers, show positive charges at neutral pH because they have amine groups (Shi et al., 2017). However, NAC has a carboxyl group, which generates negative charges at neutral pH. From this result, the peptide can absorb onto the surface of NAC-CQDs by the electrostatic interaction, which then facilitates the fluorescence quenching by the electron transfer from the NAC-QDs to peptides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between NAC-CQDs and histamine binding peptide candidates
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum of NAC-CQDs change with the added different peptides was tested. The fluorescence intensity of the NAC-CQDs could not be changed in the presence of different concentrations of histamine (Fig. 2 a). While the emission of the NAC-CQDs was quenched in the presence of the four peptides (Fig. 2b–e), and there was an enhancement in the emission after adding histamine to different degrees.

In this study, the fluorescence quenching between NAC-CQDs and peptide is responsible for the interruption of electron transfer from NAC-CQDs to peptides, leading to the negligible fluorescence change. To explore the interaction between NAC-CQDs and peptides, many analyses were performed. Based on the zeta potential analysis, NAC-CQDs showed a strong negative charge at first due to the carboxyl group on it, but the zeta potential turned positive after the addition of different peptides (Fig. S6). From which, we can deduce that the quenching effect may be due to the electrostatic effect or hydrogen bond between carboxyl groups and hydrophilic residues of peptides. It can be seen from the less quenching effect of Hisp2 toward NAC-CQDs as well. Furthermore, we changed ionic strength to distinguish the binding modes between peptides and NAC-CQDs, since the high ionic strength is favorable to the hydrophobic process but detrimental to the electrostatic binding (Shi et al., 2018). In order to explore the binding effect between them, the fluorescence intensity change was compared under different NaCl concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of NAC-CQDs did not change with the change in NaCl concentration (Fig. S7). Conversely, the fluorescence was less quenched under higher ionic concentration. These differences are attributed to the electrostatic interactions between them.

Many studies showed that most of the ligand-binding sites in peptides are hydrophobic and hydrophobic interactions constitute the major thermodynamic driving force for the binding of small-molecule ligands to peptides. Therefore, the binding of histamine to the hydrophobic domain of the target peptide would bring peptide escape from NAC-CQDs, recovering the fluorescence (Maity et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test to evaluate candidate peptides
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of NAC-CQDs and peptides system indicated a striking increase in the presence of histamine, in contrast to equivalent concentrations of the other structurally similar compounds (Fig. 3 ), illustrating the selectivity of this proposed assay. Comparing all the selected peptides for selectivity towards histamine, expect Hisp2, other peptides showed good selectivity toward histamine. Among them, the peptide also shows a little response toward cadaverine. Considering that the pKa values between histamine and cadaverine are similar, it supports that the primary amine group in histamine possesses a pivotal role as a recognition site for bioreceptors (Dwidar and Yokobayashi, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity test to evaluate candidate peptides

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensities of NAC-CQDs in PBS solution with diverse pH conditions were compared to improve the stability of the developed biosensors. As shown in Fig. S9, the NAC-CQDs-based histamine sensor exhibited excellent pH tolerance; the maximum fluorescence emission intensity of NAC-CQDs appeared at higher pH values. In the presence of Hisp3, the fluorescence intensity of NAC-CQDs showed a rapid decrease at pHs 6 and 7 and then increased with histamine adding. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity remained its signal stably with the pH range of 6–11, indicating that the biosensor has good stability in a wide pH range.

According to the above study, we found peptide Hisp3 showed the best selectivity and sensitivity effect to histamine, which may due to its specific structural caused a flexible link required for efficient binding of the histamine. To test the specific sequence influence to histamine, cysteine (Cys) incorporated (Hisp3-C, DIDRAGKASHWPC) and two repeats of sequence with Cys incorporated (Hisp3-2-C, DIDRAGKASHWPDIDRAGKASHWPC) were synthesized. The sensitivity of Hisp3-2-C showed a lower LOD of 13.0 ppb, and the selectivity test still keeps well. This result also indicates the specific sequence has an excellent affinity to histamine (Fig. S10).

Successful Au coating was confirmed through XRD and absorbance. Fig. S11a shows XRD patterns of MNP and MNP@Au. The peaks from MNP were indexed to the (311), (440), (220), and (511) planes, which correspond to Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 19.0629). On the other hand, MNP@AuNPs showed sharp peaks from the gold shell, which can be indexed to the (200), (220), and (111) planes of standard gold hydride well with reference to the JCPDS No. 04–0784. These results indicate the successful formation of MNP@Au magnet nanocomposites. Optical properties of particles suspended in water were compared via UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. S11b). By comparing the MNP, a surface plasmon resonance phenomenon causes absorption in the spectrum (~552 nm) peak when a layer of Au NPs was attached onto the MNP surface. Which revealed that the fabrication procedure of the MNP@Au NPs was successfully performed as designed (Wang et al., 2015). It possesses a specific peak at 290 nm, which due to the aromatic amino acid residues in the peptide. These results can be shown the absorption of the peptide on the magnetic nanoparticles.

At the same time, the morphology and structure of nanoparticles were investigated by FE-SEM and DLS, respectively (Fig. S12). The mean diameter of the MNP determined by SEM imaging and DLS analysis was 164.8 ± 36.5 nm (Fig. S12d). In the meanwhile, we could observe the combination of Au, Fe, and O elements in the MNP@Au NPs from the Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. S12c), which suggested the successful conjugation between Au NPs and MNP. The particle diameter was subsequently increased to 197.7 ± 56.2 nm with Au NPs coating using DLS analysis. From the zeta potential analysis (Fig. S12e), there is a positive charge on the surface of MNP as the PEI functionalization during preparation, and it reversed to −31.66 mV after Au NPs coating. Furthermore, the conjugation with Hisp3-2-C leads to a final mean size increased to 200.8 ± 35.9 nm and the zeta potential changed to −27.97 mV (Wang et al., 2015).

By quantification of protein adsorption of supernatant in an indirect BCA (bicinchoninic acid) protein assay. When an adequate concentration (0.4 mg/mL) of the Hisp3-2-C was used for testing, the ratio of added peptide and tends to be stabilized with Hisp3-2-C added and finally the concentration of Hisp3-2-C is 0.1 mg/mL (Fig. S13). Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra were utilized to confirm MNP@Au nanocomposites conjugated with histamine. From Fig. S14, the presence of histamine can be revealed at 1643.8 cm−1 and 952.5 cm−1 by the CC stretching mode and C–H out of plane bending mode of histamine. The peaks at 1633.2 cm−1 and 1101.7 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl group (CO) and amide groups due to the peptide on the surface (Mao et al., 2019).

To test the possibility of our method in detection of histamine in real samples, a black fish sample, which naturally contains histamine, was quantified via this system. Histamine was isolated following the optimized procedure using MNP@Au and Hisp3-2-C magnet nanocomposites. The isolated histamine was mixed with NAC-CQDs and Hisp3. It converted the binding events to fluorescent signals. The concentration of the histamine was then determined by interpolating the measured fluorescence intensity using the linear calibration plot (Fig. S15) and indicated by the results as shown in Table S4. The result (n = 3) achieved with the proposed method was equivalent statistically with the one achieved by HPLC. The MNP@Au and Hisp3-2-C magnet nanocomposites system showed promising potential in sensitive histamine detection in fish. Fifty mg/kg of histamine levels in fish is considered as a caution level. Hence, this method can serve as a tool for histamine detection to ensure food safety. For the comparison, the LODs of all discovered peptides were summarized in Table S5. And also, other previously used extraction methods in the literature are provided in Table 1. As seen from the results, the LOD obtained from the proposed method was significantly lower. The separation method in our system is more convenient and effective.

In summary, specific peptides which can specifically bind to histamine were selected by phage display methods. To verify their binding capability, by measuring the increase of the fluorescence intensity emitted from NAC-CQDs and peptide, it was possible to indirectly quantify the amount of histamine. Taking advantage of the NAC-CQDs and peptides system, histamine was proved to be assayed at a low concentration ranging from 0.1 to 100 ppm with a LOD of 13.0 ppb. This biosensor can be applied in histamine determination in fish samples, which showed promise as a tool to ensure food safety and maintain human health.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Rongjia Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing, Writing - review & editing. Shuaihui Feng: Methodology, Visualization. Chan Yeong Park: Data curation, Methodology, Writing, Writing - review & editing. Kyung Yeol Park: Methodology. Jeonghun Song: Methodology. Jong Pil Park: Resources, Investigation. Hyang Sook Chun: Resources, Data curation. Tae Jung Park: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Writing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from the Ministry of Food and Drug Safety (17162MFDS065). This research was further supported by the Chung-Ang University Young Scientist Scholarship in 2018.
